So a stub is a lossless, tunable reactance: when you need a specific $+jX$ or $-jX$ to cancel a load's reactive part, you reach for a stub of the right length. This is the foundation of single- and double-stub matching networks, the topic of the next module's Smith-chart work.

items.front: Write the lossless input-impedance formula for a line of length $l$ terminated in $Z_L$.
items.back: $Z_{in}(l) = Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan\beta l}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan\beta l}$.
items.hint: $Z_0$ times (load + j Z0 tan) over (Z0 + j load tan).

items.front: What does a quarter-wave ($\lambda/4$) line do to the load impedance?
items.back: It inverts it: $Z_{in} = Z_0^2 / Z_L$. A high load looks low and vice versa.
items.hint: $\tan(\pi/2) \to \infty$ in the formula.

items.front: How do you size a quarter-wave transformer to match real impedances $Z_S$ and $Z_L$?
items.back: Choose the section's characteristic impedance $Z_0 = \sqrt{Z_S Z_L}$ (geometric mean) and make it $\lambda/4$ long.
items.hint: Geometric mean of the two impedances.

items.front: What is the input impedance of a half-wavelength ($\lambda/2$) line?
items.back: $Z_{in} = Z_L$. A half-wave line repeats the load impedance exactly, independent of $Z_0$. Impedance repeats every $\lambda/2$.

items.front: Give the input reactance of a shorted stub and an open stub of length $l$.
items.back: Shorted: $Z_{in} = jZ_0\tan\beta l$ (inductive/capacitive by length). Open: $Z_{in} = -jZ_0\cot\beta l$. Both are pure reactances on a lossless line.
items.hint: Short uses tan, open uses cot (with a minus sign).

items.front: Why is a stub useful in a matching network?
items.back: It is a lossless, length-tunable reactance. By choosing its length you produce exactly the $+jX$ or $-jX$ needed to cancel a load's reactive part.
items.hint: Tunable reactance with no resistor needed.

items.prompt: To match a 100 Ω load to a 50 Ω line with a quarter-wave transformer, the section's $Z_0$ should be:
items.explanation: $Z_0 = \sqrt{Z_S Z_L} = \sqrt{50\cdot100} = \sqrt{5000} \approx 70.7\,\Omega$.

items.prompt: A 25 Ω load is seen through a $\lambda/4$ section of 50 Ω line. The input impedance is:
items.explanation: $Z_{in} = Z_0^2/Z_L = 50^2/25 = 2500/25 = 100\,\Omega$. The quarter-wave line inverts the load about $Z_0$.

items.prompt: Looking into a $\lambda/2$ line terminated in a complex load $Z_L = 30 + j40\ \Omega$ on a 50 Ω line, the input impedance is:
items.explanation: A half-wave line repeats the load exactly: $Z_{in} = Z_L = 30 + j40\ \Omega$, regardless of $Z_0$.

items.prompt: A shorted lossless stub presents what kind of input impedance?
choices: A complex impedance with equal real and imaginary parts
items.explanation: With $Z_L = 0$, the formula gives $Z_{in} = jZ_0\tan\beta l$ — a purely imaginary (reactive) input, tunable via length.

items.prompt: A shorted stub one-eighth wavelength long ($\beta l = \pi/4$) on a 50 Ω line presents:
items.explanation: $Z_{in} = j50\tan(\pi/4) = j50\cdot1 = +j50\ \Omega$ — an inductive reactance.

items.prompt: Why does input impedance vary with position along a mismatched line?
choices: Because the reflected wave's phase rotates as it travels, changing the V/I ratio
items.explanation: The incident and reflected waves combine with a position-dependent relative phase ($\beta l$), so their voltage-to-current ratio — the input impedance — rotates with distance.

lessons.title: Why Match: Power Transfer, Reflections & VSWR
lessons.content:
Before any chart, fix *why* matching matters. Three reasons, all things you metered as a radar tech.

**Maximum power transfer.** A source with internal impedance $Z_S$ delivers the most power into a load when $Z_L = Z_S^*$ (the complex conjugate). For a real $50\,\Omega$ system that just means $Z_L = 50\,\Omega$. Mismatch wastes power as reflection rather than delivering it to the antenna.

**Minimize reflections.** On a line of characteristic impedance $Z_0$, a load reflects a wave with reflection coefficient $\Gamma_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$. A matched load ($Z_L = Z_0$) gives $\Gamma_L = 0$: nothing comes back. Reflected power can desensitize receivers, cause amplitude/phase ripple, and at high power can damage the source.

**VSWR.** The forward and reflected waves interfere to form a standing wave whose voltage swings between maxima and minima. The ratio is
$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + |\Gamma|}{1 - |\Gamma|}.$$
A perfect match is VSWR $= 1{:}1$ ($|\Gamma|=0$); a total reflection (open/short) is $\infty{:}1$ ($|\Gamma|=1$). Return loss in dB is $\text{RL} = -20\log_{10}|\Gamma|$.

Rule-of-thumb anchors worth memorizing: VSWR $2{:}1 \Rightarrow |\Gamma| \approx 0.33$, RL $\approx 9.5$ dB, about $11\%$ of power reflected. VSWR $1.5{:}1 \Rightarrow |\Gamma| = 0.2$, RL $\approx 14$ dB. These four quantities — $Z$, $\Gamma$, VSWR, RL — are interchangeable descriptions of one mismatch, and the whole point of the Smith chart is to read all of them off a single plot.

items.front: For maximum power transfer, what should the load impedance equal?
items.back: The complex conjugate of the source impedance, $Z_L = Z_S^*$. In a real $50\,\Omega$ system this is simply $Z_L = 50\,\Omega$.
items.hint: It's a conjugate, not just equality.

items.front: Write the load reflection coefficient in terms of $Z_L$ and $Z_0$.
items.back: $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$. It is zero when $Z_L = Z_0$ (matched) and has magnitude 1 for a pure open or short.
items.hint: Difference over sum, normalized by $Z_0$.

items.front: Give the VSWR formula in terms of $|\Gamma|$.
items.back: $\text{VSWR} = \dfrac{1 + |\Gamma|}{1 - |\Gamma|}$. Range is $1$ (matched) to $\infty$ (total reflection).
items.hint: One plus over one minus.

items.front: How is return loss (dB) related to $|\Gamma|$?
items.back: $\text{RL} = -20\log_{10}|\Gamma|$ dB. Larger RL means a better match (less reflected). RL $= \infty$ for a perfect match.
items.hint: It is a voltage ratio, so $20\log$.

items.front: What |\Gamma|, return loss, and reflected-power fraction correspond to VSWR 2:1?
items.back: $|\Gamma| \approx 0.33$, return loss $\approx 9.5$ dB, and about $11\%$ ($|\Gamma|^2$) of incident power reflected.
items.hint: Reflected power fraction is $|\Gamma|^2$.

items.prompt: A $50\,\Omega$ line is terminated in $100\,\Omega$ (purely real). What is $|\Gamma_L|$?
Which condition gives the lowest VSWR?
items.explanation: $Z_L = Z_0$ makes $\Gamma = 0$ and VSWR $= 1{:}1$. Open, short, and $|\Gamma|=1$ all give infinite VSWR.

items.prompt: Roughly what fraction of incident power is reflected at VSWR $1.5{:}1$ ($|\Gamma| = 0.2$)?
items.explanation: Reflected power fraction is $|\Gamma|^2 = 0.2^2 = 0.04 = 4\%$. Return loss is about 14 dB.

items.prompt: Why is reflected power a problem at the output of a high-power transmitter?
choices: It can overheat or damage the final stage and causes ripple
items.explanation: Reflected energy returns to the source, stressing the final amplifier and creating amplitude/phase ripple; severe mismatch can destroy the device.

lessons.title: The Smith Chart as the Complex-Gamma Plane
lessons.content:
The Smith chart is nothing mysterious: it is the unit disk of the complex reflection-coefficient plane, with impedance contours printed on top. Everything follows from working in **normalized impedance** $z = Z/Z_0$ (so $z = 1$ is a perfect $50\,\Omega$ match) and the bilinear map
$$\Gamma = \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}, \qquad z = \frac{1 + \Gamma}{1 - \Gamma}.$$

Because a passive load has $|\Gamma| \le 1$, every realizable impedance lands inside the outer circle, the $|\Gamma| = 1$ boundary. Key landmarks: the **center** is $z = 1$ (matched), the **far right** on the real axis is $z = \infty$ (open, $\Gamma = +1$), the **far left** is $z = 0$ (short, $\Gamma = -1$). The horizontal axis is purely real (resistive) impedance.

The genius of the chart is that the curves of constant normalized resistance $r$ and constant normalized reactance $x$ map to **circles and arcs**. Lines of constant $r$ are circles all tangent to the right-hand point ($z=\infty$); larger $r$ gives smaller circles nested toward the right. Lines of constant $x$ are arcs emanating from that same right-hand point: the **upper half** is inductive ($+x$), the **lower half** is capacitive ($-x$). Their intersection is the point $z = r + jx$.

A fixed-$|\Gamma|$ circle centered on the chart is a **constant-VSWR circle**: rotating around it changes the phase of $\Gamma$ (i.e. moves along the line) without changing VSWR. So one plotted point simultaneously tells you $z$, $\Gamma$ (radius and angle), VSWR (radius), and return loss. That single-glance, four-quantities-at-once property is why the chart survived the calculator era.

items.front: What is normalized impedance and why use it?
items.back: $z = Z/Z_0$. Normalizing makes the chart universal: $z = 1$ is always the match regardless of whether $Z_0$ is 50, 75, or any other value.
items.hint: Divide by the system reference impedance.

items.front: Give the two-way mapping between $z$ and $\Gamma$.
items.back: $\Gamma = \dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$ and $z = \dfrac{1+\Gamma}{1-\Gamma}$. These are inverse bilinear (Mobius) transforms.
items.hint: Use normalized $z$, not raw $Z$.

items.front: Where on the Smith chart are the matched point, the open, and the short?
items.back: Center $= z = 1$ (matched, $\Gamma=0$); far right $= z=\infty$ (open, $\Gamma=+1$); far left $= z=0$ (short, $\Gamma=-1$).
items.hint: All three lie on the horizontal real axis.

items.front: What shapes do constant-$r$ and constant-$x$ contours take on the chart?
items.back: Constant-$r$ are circles all tangent at the right-hand ($z=\infty$) point; constant-$x$ are arcs radiating from that same point. Upper half is inductive ($+x$), lower half capacitive ($-x$).
items.hint: Circles for R, arcs for X.

items.front: What does a circle centered on the middle of the chart represent?
items.back: A constant-$|\Gamma|$ circle, i.e. a constant-VSWR circle. Moving along a transmission line rotates around it without changing VSWR.
items.hint: Constant radius from center = constant magnitude.

items.prompt: A normalized impedance $z = 1 + j1$ plots where on the chart?
choices: On the real axis to the right
items.explanation: $r=1$ puts it on the unit-resistance circle; $+jx$ is reactance above the real axis (inductive). This is the plotted load point in the diagram.

items.prompt: What does the outer boundary circle of the Smith chart correspond to?
items.explanation: The rim is $|\Gamma| = 1$: all reactive or fully reflecting (lossless) terminations. Passive loads lie on or inside it.

items.prompt: A point on the real axis halfway between center and the right edge has $\Gamma = +0.5$. What is its normalized impedance?
items.explanation: $z = (1+\Gamma)/(1-\Gamma) = 1.5/0.5 = 3$. Real positive $\Gamma$ corresponds to $z>1$ (resistive).

items.prompt: On the chart, the lower half of the disk represents loads that are predominantly:
items.explanation: By convention the upper half is inductive ($+x$) and the lower half is capacitive ($-x$); the real axis is purely resistive.

lessons.title: Motion Along the Line & the Admittance Chart
lessons.content:
Plotting a point is only half the chart's value; the other half is *moving*. Because $\Gamma(z) = \Gamma_L\, e^{-j2\beta l}$ as you slide distance $l$ along a lossless line, motion along the line is pure **rotation** around the constant-VSWR circle.

**Direction and amount.** Moving **toward the generator** (away from the load, back toward the source) rotates **clockwise**. Moving toward the load rotates counter-clockwise. The phase is $2\beta l = 2(2\pi/\lambda)l = 4\pi l/\lambda$, so a half-wavelength of line ($l = \lambda/2$) is a full $360^\circ$ revolution — impedance repeats every $\lambda/2$. A quarter-wavelength is $180^\circ$, which flips a point straight across the center. The chart rims are scaled in 'wavelengths toward generator' and 'toward load' precisely to count these rotations. Classic rust-knocker pitfall: rotating the wrong way, or assuming one turn is $\lambda$ instead of $\lambda/2$.

**The admittance chart.** Matching with *shunt* elements is far easier in admittance, $y = Y/Y_0 = g + jb$, because shunt elements add in admittance. The beautiful trick: on the Smith chart, the admittance $y$ corresponding to an impedance $z$ is found by a $180^\circ$ rotation about the center (it is the $\lambda/4$ point). So the *same* chart, mentally rotated half a turn, becomes a $g$-$b$ admittance chart. Many engineers overlay both — the **ZY (immittance) chart** — so series moves (constant-$r$/$x$) and shunt moves (constant-$g$/$b$) are both visible. Keep ruthless track of which chart you are on; mixing them up is the most common stub-matching error.

items.front: Which way do you rotate on the Smith chart to move toward the generator?
items.back: Clockwise. Moving toward the load is counter-clockwise. This follows from $\Gamma(l) = \Gamma_L e^{-j2\beta l}$.
items.hint: Generator = clockwise (mnemonic: 'g' before 'l' going down the clock).

items.front: How much line length equals one full revolution on the chart?
items.back: $\lambda/2$. The phase rotates by $2\beta l = 4\pi l/\lambda$, so $l = \lambda/2$ gives $360^\circ$ and the impedance pattern repeats every half wavelength.
items.hint: Not $\lambda$ — half that.

items.front: What rotation corresponds to a quarter-wavelength of line, and what does it do?
items.back: $180^\circ$ (half a turn). A $\lambda/4$ line flips a point straight across the center of the chart, transforming $z \to 1/z$ (normalized).
items.hint: Half of a full revolution.

items.front: How do you convert impedance $z$ to admittance $y$ on the Smith chart?
items.back: Rotate $180^\circ$ about the center (the $\lambda/4$ point). The new point read on the impedance grid gives $y = g + jb$.
items.hint: Same point, opposite side of center.

items.front: Why work in admittance for shunt matching elements?
items.back: Shunt elements add in admittance ($y_{tot} = y_1 + y_2$), just as series elements add in impedance. The admittance chart makes shunt moves follow constant-$g$ and constant-$b$ contours.
items.hint: Parallel = admittances add.

items.prompt: You move $0.125\lambda$ from the load toward the generator. How far around the chart, and which direction?
items.explanation: $\lambda/2$ is $360^\circ$, so $0.125\lambda = \lambda/8$ is $90^\circ$. Toward the generator is clockwise.

items.prompt: A normalized impedance $z = 0.5 + j0$ is converted to admittance by a $180^\circ$ rotation. What is $y$?
items.explanation: For a pure resistance the rotation gives $y = 1/z = 1/0.5 = 2$. Admittance is the reciprocal of impedance (normalized).

items.prompt: Why does a load's impedance repeat every half wavelength along a lossless line?
items.explanation: The reflection coefficient phase advances by $2\beta l$; at $l = \lambda/2$ that is $2\pi$, a full revolution, returning to the same point.

items.prompt: What is the combined ZY (immittance) chart used for?
choices: Overlaying impedance and admittance grids so series and shunt moves are both visible
items.explanation: The ZY chart superimposes the constant-$r$/$x$ (series) and constant-$g$/$b$ (shunt) grids, so you can switch between series and shunt element moves without re-plotting.

lessons.title: Matching Networks: L-Networks, Stubs, Transformers & Q
lessons.content:
Now the payoff: dragging an arbitrary load to the center ($z=1$). Several standard tools, each a path on the chart.

**Lumped L-network.** Two reactive elements (one series, one shunt) in an 'L'. On the chart you alternate a constant-$r$ move (series element) and a constant-$g$ move (shunt element) until you reach the center. There are generally **two solutions** per load. A useful split: if the load is *outside* the $r=1$ circle, start with a series element; if *inside*, start with a shunt element. Series $L$ moves up the reactance arc, series $C$ down; shunt $C$ moves up the susceptance arc, shunt $L$ down. The figure shows a series-$L$ / shunt-$C$ topology.

**Single-stub tuning.** Run a length of line until the load admittance sits on the $g=1$ circle, then cancel the leftover susceptance $\pm jb$ with a shorted or open stub of the right length. **Double-stub** uses two fixed-position stubs (no need to cut the main line), trading flexibility for a forbidden region.

**Quarter-wave transformer.** To match two real impedances, insert a $\lambda/4$ line of $Z_0' = \sqrt{Z_0 Z_L}$ — the same $\sqrt{\eta_1\eta_2}$ trick from optics. Simple but inherently narrowband.

**Bandwidth and Q.** A match is exact at one frequency. The loaded $Q \approx \sqrt{(R_{high}/R_{low}) - 1}$ for an L-network; higher transformation ratio means higher $Q$ and *narrower* bandwidth. The **Bode-Fano limit** sets the fundamental ceiling on how broadband any passive match of a reactive load can be — you cannot match perfectly over all frequencies. For wider bandwidth, use multi-section transformers or lower-$Q$ multi-element networks. Modern workflow: confirm by hand on the chart, then verify in **scikit-rf** or on a **VNA**.

items.front: What is an L-network and how many solutions usually exist?
items.back: Two reactive elements (one series, one shunt) arranged in an 'L'. There are generally two valid topologies/solutions that match a given load to $Z_0$.
items.hint: Two elements, two answers.

items.front: For an L-network, when do you start with a series vs a shunt element?
items.back: If the load lies outside the $r=1$ circle, start with a series element; if inside, start with a shunt element. This gets you onto the $r=1$ or $g=1$ circle for the second element to finish.
items.hint: Outside $r=1$ -> series first.

items.front: Describe the single-stub matching procedure on the chart.
items.back: Move along the line (rotate) until the load admittance reaches the $g=1$ circle, then add a shorted/open stub whose susceptance cancels the residual $\pm jb$, landing at the center.
items.hint: Reach $g=1$, then cancel $b$.

items.front: Give the characteristic impedance of a quarter-wave transformer matching $Z_0$ to a real $Z_L$.
items.back: $Z_0' = \sqrt{Z_0 Z_L}$, a line exactly $\lambda/4$ long. It is the transmission-line analog of the $\sqrt{\eta_1\eta_2}$ optical matching layer; inherently narrowband.
items.hint: Geometric mean of the two impedances.

items.front: How does the transformation ratio of a match relate to bandwidth?
items.back: Higher ratio means higher loaded $Q$ (for an L-network $Q \approx \sqrt{R_{high}/R_{low} - 1}$) and therefore narrower bandwidth. The Bode-Fano limit caps how broadband any passive match can be.
items.hint: Bigger jump -> higher Q -> narrower.

items.front: What is the Bode-Fano limit?
items.back: A fundamental theoretical bound on the best reflection-coefficient bandwidth product achievable when matching a reactive (e.g. RC or RL) load with any passive lossless network. You cannot match a reactive load perfectly over all frequencies.
items.hint: Sets the broadband matching ceiling.

items.prompt: Match $Z_L = 100\,\Omega$ (real) to $50\,\Omega$ with a quarter-wave transformer. What $Z_0'$ is required?
items.explanation: $Z_0' = \sqrt{50 \times 100} = \sqrt{5000} \approx 70.7\,\Omega$.

items.prompt: In an L-network matching a load inside the $r=1$ circle to $50\,\Omega$, the correct first move is to add a:
items.explanation: Loads inside the $r=1$ circle are reached by a shunt element first (constant-$g$ move), then a series element completes the match.

items.prompt: A single-stub tuner uses the stub primarily to:
choices: Cancel the residual susceptance once the line brings you to the $g=1$ circle
items.explanation: The line length moves the admittance onto the $g=1$ circle; the stub supplies a pure $\pm jb$ to cancel the leftover susceptance and reach the center.

items.prompt: Which statement about quarter-wave transformer bandwidth is correct?
choices: It is inherently narrowband because the $\lambda/4$ condition holds at only one frequency
Its bandwidth grows with transformation ratio
It works only for complex loads
items.explanation: The $\lambda/4$ electrical length is exact at the design frequency only, so the single-section transformer is inherently narrowband; larger ratios make it worse, and multi-section designs broaden it.

items.prompt: Which modern tool would you use to verify a hand Smith-chart match numerically, leveraging a software background?
items.explanation: scikit-rf handles network/S-parameter analysis and Smith-chart plotting in Python, ideal for confirming a by-hand match; a VNA validates it on real hardware.

lessons.title: Why Hollow Waveguides, and Why No TEM
lessons.content:
You already know this hardware from the radar feed deck: a polished brass or aluminum rectangular tube carrying X-band power to the antenna. Now reconnect it to the field theory. **Why use a hollow pipe instead of coax at microwave and radar frequencies?** Two reasons. First, **low loss**: coax loss climbs steeply with frequency because the center conductor is thin and skin depth shrinks, concentrating current and $I^2R$ dissipation. A hollow guide has no center conductor, so the current spreads over large wall area and ohmic loss stays modest. Second, **high power handling**: with no thin inner conductor and no solid dielectric, there is far less to arc or burn, so a guide handles the kilowatts a radar transmitter pushes.

The key structural fact: a **single-conductor hollow guide cannot carry a TEM (transverse electromagnetic) wave**. TEM needs two conductors (like coax inner and outer) so a static-like transverse voltage can exist between them. Inside a hollow pipe there is no second conductor and no DC path, so any propagating field must have a longitudinal component, either $E_z$ or $H_z$. That forces the supported solutions into two families: **TE modes** (transverse electric, $E_z = 0$, $H_z \neq 0$) and **TM modes** (transverse magnetic, $H_z = 0$, $E_z \neq 0$).

The boundary conditions do the rest: tangential $E$ must vanish at the conducting walls. That requirement quantizes the field into discrete mode patterns indexed by integers $(m, n)$. Each mode has its own field shape and, as the next lesson shows, its own cutoff frequency below which it simply cannot propagate.

items.front: Why are hollow waveguides preferred over coax at microwave/radar frequencies?
items.back: Lower loss (no thin center conductor, so current spreads over large wall area and skin-effect dissipation is reduced) and higher power handling (no thin inner conductor or solid dielectric to arc or burn).
items.hint: Think about the center conductor and skin effect.

items.front: Why can't a hollow single-conductor waveguide support a TEM wave?
items.back: TEM requires two conductors to sustain a transverse voltage (like coax inner/outer). A single hollow conductor has no second conductor, so any propagating wave must have a longitudinal field component, giving only TE or TM modes.
items.hint: How many conductors does TEM need?

items.front: Define TE and TM modes by their longitudinal field components.
items.back: TE (transverse electric): $E_z = 0$, $H_z \neq 0$. TM (transverse magnetic): $H_z = 0$, $E_z \neq 0$.
items.hint: Which field is zero along the propagation axis?

items.front: What physical boundary condition forces waveguide fields into discrete modes?
items.back: Tangential electric field must vanish at the conducting walls. Satisfying this on all walls quantizes the transverse field pattern into discrete modes indexed by integers (m, n).
items.hint: What must E do at a perfect conductor surface?

items.front: What do the mode indices (m, n) physically count in a rectangular guide?
items.back: The number of half-wave field variations across the two transverse dimensions: m half-cycles along the broad wall (a) and n along the narrow wall (b).
items.hint: Half-sine variations along each dimension.

items.prompt: A standard coax line supports a TEM mode but a hollow rectangular waveguide does not. The fundamental reason is:
choices: Coax has two conductors that can sustain a transverse voltage; the hollow guide has only one
Coax has lower loss than a waveguide at all frequencies
The waveguide dielectric blocks transverse electric fields
TEM modes require a magnetic core, present only in coax
items.explanation: TEM needs two conductors to support a purely transverse field with no longitudinal component. A single hollow conductor cannot, so it supports only TE and TM modes.

items.prompt: In a high-power X-band radar transmit chain, waveguide is often chosen over coax mainly because:
choices: Waveguide is cheaper to manufacture than coax
Waveguide has no thin center conductor or solid dielectric to arc or overheat, so it handles high power with lower loss
Coax cannot pass frequencies above 1 GHz at all
Waveguide is immune to reflections and never needs matching
items.explanation: The absence of a thin inner conductor and solid dielectric lets the guide carry kilowatts with low loss; coax loss and breakdown become limiting at these power levels and frequencies.

items.prompt: A mode in a rectangular guide has $E_z = 0$ everywhere but $H_z \neq 0$. This mode is:
items.explanation: Zero longitudinal electric field with nonzero longitudinal magnetic field defines a transverse-electric (TE) mode.

items.prompt: Why does coax loss rise more steeply with frequency than waveguide loss?
choices: Coax dielectric becomes a perfect conductor at high frequency
Skin effect concentrates current on the thin center conductor, raising effective resistance and I-squared-R loss
Waveguides radiate the lost power back into the line
Coax has no skin effect, so all loss is radiated
items.explanation: As frequency rises, skin depth shrinks and current crowds onto the small surface of the thin center conductor, increasing resistive loss. A hollow guide spreads current over large wall area.

lessons.title: Cutoff, the TE10 Dominant Mode, and Guide Wavelength
lessons.content:
Every waveguide mode has a **cutoff frequency** below which it cannot propagate. For a rectangular guide of broad dimension $a$ and narrow dimension $b$:
$$f_c = \frac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2}$$
The corresponding cutoff wavelength is $\lambda_c = 2/\sqrt{(m/a)^2 + (n/b)^2}$. A guide therefore behaves like a **high-pass filter**: above $f_c$ a mode propagates; below it the fields are **evanescent**, decaying exponentially with no real power transport.

The lowest-cutoff mode in a rectangular guide is **$TE_{10}$**, the **dominant mode**. With $m=1, n=0$ the formula collapses to $f_c = c/(2a)$, so $\lambda_c = 2a$ depends only on the broad wall. $TE_{10}$ is preferred because it has the lowest cutoff, a simple field pattern (a single half-sine of $E$ across $a$, uniform along $b$), and a wide single-mode band before $TE_{20}$ or $TE_{01}$ turns on. This is exactly why WR-90 (a $\approx 0.9$ in) is the X-band standard: its $TE_{10}$ cutoff sits below the band, and the next mode sits above it.

Once above cutoff, the wave travels with a **guide wavelength** longer than free space:
$$\lambda_g = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$$
As $f \to f_c$, $\lambda_g \to \infty$; as $f \gg f_c$, $\lambda_g \to \lambda$. This stretched wavelength is what you measured on a slotted line when locating standing-wave minima in the feed.

items.front: Rectangular waveguide cutoff frequency formula?
items.back: $f_c = \frac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2}$, where a and b are the broad and narrow inner dimensions and (m,n) are the mode indices.
items.hint: Half the speed of light times a root of squared index/dimension ratios.

items.front: What does the cutoff formula reduce to for the TE10 mode, and its cutoff wavelength?
items.back: With m=1, n=0: $f_c = c/(2a)$ and $\lambda_c = 2a$. It depends only on the broad-wall dimension a.
items.hint: Set n = 0 in the general formula.

items.front: What happens to a mode below its cutoff frequency?
items.back: It becomes evanescent: the fields decay exponentially along the guide with no real power propagation. The guide acts as a high-pass filter for that mode.
items.hint: No propagation, exponential decay.

items.front: Guide wavelength formula and its limiting behavior?
items.back: $\lambda_g = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$. As $f \to f_c$, $\lambda_g \to \infty$; as $f \gg f_c$, $\lambda_g \to \lambda$. The guide wavelength is always longer than free-space $\lambda$.
items.hint: Free-space lambda divided by a root involving fc/f.

items.front: Why is TE10 the chosen operating mode in rectangular guides?
items.back: It has the lowest cutoff frequency, a simple single-half-sine field pattern, and the widest single-mode operating band before the next mode (TE20 or TE01) starts to propagate.
items.hint: Lowest cutoff, simplest pattern, widest clean band.

items.prompt: With respect to frequency, a waveguide mode behaves like which kind of filter?
items.explanation: A mode propagates only above its cutoff frequency and is evanescent below it, which is exactly high-pass behavior.

items.prompt: A rectangular guide has broad dimension a = 2 cm. The TE10 cutoff frequency is approximately:
Operating above cutoff, how does the guide wavelength compare to the free-space wavelength at the same frequency?
items.explanation: Since the denominator sqrt(1-(fc/f)^2) is less than 1, lambda_g exceeds the free-space lambda, approaching it only far above cutoff.

items.prompt: WR-90 waveguide is sized for X-band because:
choices: Its TE10 cutoff lies below the band while the next higher mode's cutoff lies above it, giving a clean single-mode band
It is the only guide that supports TEM at X-band
Its dimensions make all modes cut off simultaneously
It maximizes loss to attenuate unwanted signals
items.explanation: Standard WR sizes are chosen so the dominant TE10 propagates across the band while the next mode stays cut off, ensuring single-mode operation.

lessons.title: Phase vs Group Velocity, Other Guides, and Practical Hardware
lessons.content:
Inside a guide the wave zig-zags down the pipe, which makes the **phase velocity exceed c** while the **group velocity stays below c**. They satisfy $v_p \, v_g = c^2$, with
$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}, \qquad v_g = c\,\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}.$$
Don't panic at $v_p > c$: phase velocity carries no information. Energy and signals travel at $v_g$, so causality is safe. Because $v_p$ and $v_g$ depend on $f/f_c$, a guide is inherently **dispersive**: different frequencies travel at different speeds, spreading a pulse. Near cutoff this dispersion is severe, which is one reason radars operate well above $f_c$.

**Rectangular vs circular guides:** rectangular guides with $TE_{10}$ have a fixed polarization and are the workhorse. Circular guides ($TE_{11}$ dominant) carry more power and lower loss in some modes ($TE_{01}$ for long runs) but allow polarization rotation, so they appear in rotary joints feeding rotating radar antennas. **Dielectric/optical waveguides** flip the mechanism: instead of conducting walls, a high-index core guides light by total internal reflection, the same boundary physics scaled to optical frequencies, the basis of fiber.

**Practical hardware** you handled: **flanges** (choke and cover) bolt sections together with low leakage; **irises** (inductive or capacitive posts/windows) and **tuning screws** add reactance for matching, the waveguide equivalent of stubs; **bends and tees** route power; and **coax-to-waveguide transitions** use a probe (a coax center pin acting as a small monopole) inserted a quarter guide-wavelength from a shorted back wall to launch $TE_{10}$. **Substrate-integrated waveguide (SIW)** recreates these guides in PCB form with via fences, bringing waveguide behavior to modern planar mmWave boards.

items.front: How do phase and group velocity relate in a waveguide?
items.back: $v_p \, v_g = c^2$, with $v_p = c/\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2}$ and $v_g = c\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^2}$. So $v_p > c$ and $v_g < c$.
items.hint: Their product is c squared.

items.front: Why is a phase velocity greater than c not a causality violation in a waveguide?
items.back: Phase velocity carries no information; it is just how fast a constant-phase point moves. Energy and signals travel at the group velocity, which is always less than c.
items.hint: Which velocity actually carries information?

items.front: Why is a hollow waveguide dispersive?
items.back: Both phase and group velocity depend on f/fc, so different frequency components travel at different speeds, spreading a pulse. Dispersion is worst near cutoff.
items.hint: Velocity depends on f/fc.

items.front: How does a typical coax-to-waveguide transition launch the TE10 mode?
items.back: A coax center-conductor probe (a small monopole) is inserted into the broad wall about a quarter guide-wavelength from a shorted end wall, so the reflected wave adds in phase and excites TE10.
items.hint: A probe and a shorted back wall a quarter-lambda-g away.

items.front: What is the function of irises and tuning screws in a waveguide?
items.back: They add shunt reactance (inductive or capacitive) for impedance matching, serving as the waveguide equivalent of transmission-line stubs.
items.hint: Waveguide version of a matching stub.

items.front: Where do circular waveguides show up in radar feeds and why?
items.back: In rotary joints feeding rotating antennas: a circular guide's symmetry allows the field to pass through a rotating interface, and some modes (TE01) offer very low loss for long runs.
items.hint: Rotating antenna feeds.

items.prompt: A waveguide measurement shows phase velocity greater than the speed of light. The correct interpretation is:
choices: The measurement is impossible and must be an error
Information is traveling faster than light, violating relativity
Phase velocity carries no information; energy travels at group velocity, which is below c
The guide is operating below cutoff
items.explanation: Phase velocity exceeding c is normal in guides. Signals and energy propagate at the group velocity (< c), so causality holds.

items.prompt: If a waveguide's group velocity at the operating frequency is 0.6c, the phase velocity is:
A dielectric/optical waveguide (optical fiber) confines a wave primarily by:
choices: Total internal reflection at the core-cladding boundary due to the index difference
The cutoff high-pass behavior of a hollow metal pipe
Magnetic confinement from an external coil
items.explanation: Optical fiber has no conducting walls; a higher-index core guides light by total internal reflection at the core-cladding interface.

items.prompt: Substrate-integrated waveguide (SIW) is best described as:
choices: A waveguide formed inside a PCB using rows of plated vias as side walls, bringing waveguide behavior to planar boards
A coaxial cable with extra shielding
A microstrip line with no ground plane
An optical fiber embedded in a substrate
items.explanation: SIW uses metal via fences in a dielectric substrate to emulate the walls of a rectangular waveguide, enabling waveguide-like performance in planar mmWave PCB designs.

lessons.title: Radiation Mechanism & Field Regions
lessons.content:
An antenna converts a guided wave on a transmission line into a free-space wave (and vice versa). The physics comes straight from Module 2: **radiation requires acceleration of charge**, equivalently a *time-varying current*. A steady current makes a static magnetic field that stays bound to the wire; a current that changes in time produces a $\partial \mathbf{D}/\partial t$ displacement term whose fields detach and propagate. No acceleration, no radiation.

The canonical radiator is the **infinitesimal (Hertzian) dipole**, a current element $I\,dl \ll \lambda$. Build a practical antenna from it and you get the **half-wave dipole** ($L = \lambda/2$), where a standing current wave peaks at the feed and goes to zero at the tips.

Around any antenna the space divides into three regions by distance:

- **Reactive near field** ($r < 0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$): energy sloshes back and forth, stored not radiated; fields drop faster than $1/r$.
- **Radiating near field (Fresnel)**: power radiates but the pattern still changes shape with distance.
- **Far field (Fraunhofer)** ($r > 2D^2/\lambda$): the pattern is fixed, fields fall as $1/r$, and the wave looks locally planar.

Here $D$ is the largest antenna dimension. For your old radar dishes this matters operationally: you cannot measure true gain or pattern while standing inside the near field. A 1 m dish at 10 GHz ($\lambda = 0.03$ m) has a far-field distance $2D^2/\lambda \approx 667$ m, which is why antenna ranges and calibration towers are physically large.

items.front: What is the fundamental requirement for an antenna to radiate?
items.back: Acceleration of charge, equivalently a time-varying current. A constant (DC) current produces only a static, bound magnetic field and does not radiate.
items.hint: Think about why displacement current matters.

items.front: What is the Hertzian (infinitesimal) dipole and why is it useful?
items.back: A current element $I\,dl$ much shorter than a wavelength, with uniform current. It is the canonical building block: any real antenna's field can be built by superposing such elements.
items.hint: Canonical radiator.

items.front: Name the three field regions around an antenna, near to far.
items.back: Reactive near field, radiating near field (Fresnel), and far field (Fraunhofer).
items.hint: Reactive, Fresnel, Fraunhofer.

items.front: What is the far-field (Fraunhofer) boundary distance in terms of antenna size and wavelength?
items.back: $r > \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$, where $D$ is the largest antenna dimension. Beyond this the pattern shape is fixed and fields fall as $1/r$.
items.hint: Quadratic in D, scaled by lambda.

items.front: Describe the current distribution on a half-wave dipole.
items.back: Roughly sinusoidal: maximum at the center feed point and zero at the two open tips, since current cannot flow off the ends.
items.hint: Where is current forced to zero?

items.prompt: A 1 m parabolic dish operates at 10 GHz ($\lambda \approx 0.03$ m). Approximately how far away does the far field begin?
items.explanation: $r = 2D^2/\lambda = 2(1)^2/0.03 \approx 66.7/0.1 \approx 667$ m. This large distance is why true gain and pattern can only be measured on a long antenna range or by near-field scanning.

items.prompt: In the reactive near field of an antenna, the energy is primarily:
choices: Radiating away as a plane wave
Stored and exchanged with the antenna, not radiated
Converted to heat in free space
Traveling at greater than the speed of light
items.explanation: The reactive near field holds stored (reactive) energy that sloshes between the antenna and surrounding space without net radiation; these fields decay faster than $1/r$.

items.prompt: Why does a half-wave dipole radiate while a DC-fed wire does not?
choices: The dipole carries a time-varying current, producing detaching (radiating) fields
DC current cannot exist on a wire
The dipole is exactly one wavelength long
items.explanation: Radiation requires accelerating charge, i.e. a time-varying current. The DC wire has a static bound field; the RF-fed dipole's changing current launches fields that detach and propagate.

items.prompt: In the far-field criterion $r > 2D^2/\lambda$, what does $D$ represent?
choices: The largest physical dimension of the antenna
The diameter of the feed line
items.explanation: $D$ is the largest physical aperture dimension (e.g. dish diameter or dipole length). Do not confuse it with directivity, which is also often written $D$.

lessons.title: Radiation Pattern, Directivity, Gain & Efficiency
lessons.content:
The **radiation pattern** plots relative radiated power versus angle. It has a **main lobe** pointing at boresight, **side lobes** (unwanted), **back lobes**, and **nulls** (directions of near-zero radiation). Two beamwidths matter: the **half-power beamwidth (HPBW)**, the angular width between the $-3$ dB points of the main lobe, and the **first-null beamwidth (FNBW)**. For a radar, HPBW directly sets angular resolution.

**Directivity** $D$ measures how concentrated the radiation is relative to an isotropic radiator: $D = \dfrac{U_{max}}{U_{avg}}$, the ratio of peak radiation intensity to the average over all directions. It is a pure geometry/pattern quantity with no loss in it.

**Gain** folds in **radiation efficiency** $\eta$ (ohmic and dielectric losses):

$$G = \eta D$$

Gain is what a link budget uses. A lossless antenna has $G = D$; a real one is lower. The half-wave dipole has $D \approx 1.64$, i.e. $2.15$ dBi, and is nearly $100\%$ efficient.

Units matter: **dBi** is gain referenced to an isotropic radiator; **dBd** is referenced to a half-wave dipole, with $\text{dBi} = \text{dBd} + 2.15$.

When you add transmit power, gain, and feed loss, you get **EIRP** (effective isotropic radiated power):

$$\text{EIRP} = P_t \, G_t \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{EIRP}_{dBm} = P_{t,dBm} - L_{line,dB} + G_{t,dBi}$$

EIRP is the apparent power an isotropic source would need to match your antenna's boresight power density. A common rust-knocker: **conflating directivity and gain** — gain always includes efficiency.

items.front: Name the key features of a radiation pattern.
items.back: Main lobe (boresight), side lobes, back lobe(s), and nulls. Front-to-back ratio compares the main lobe to the back lobe.
items.hint: Lobes and nulls.

items.front: What is the half-power beamwidth (HPBW)?
items.back: The angular width of the main lobe between the two points where radiated power falls to half (the $-3$ dB points). It sets a radar's angular resolution.
items.hint: Measured at the 3 dB points.

items.front: Define directivity D.
items.back: $D = U_{max}/U_{avg}$, the ratio of peak radiation intensity to the average radiation intensity over all directions. It is a pattern/geometry quantity with no losses included.
items.hint: Peak over average intensity.

items.front: How are gain, directivity, and efficiency related?
items.back: $G = \eta D$, where $\eta$ is the radiation efficiency (ohmic and dielectric losses). Gain is always $\le$ directivity.
items.hint: One Greek letter times directivity.

items.front: What are the directivity and gain of an ideal half-wave dipole?
items.back: $D \approx 1.64$, i.e. $2.15$ dBi. Because it is nearly lossless, its gain is essentially the same $2.15$ dBi.
items.hint: About 2.15 dBi.

items.front: What is EIRP and how is it computed in dB?
items.back: Effective Isotropic Radiated Power: $\text{EIRP} = P_t G_t$. In dB: $\text{EIRP}_{dBm} = P_{t,dBm} - L_{line,dB} + G_{t,dBi}$. It is the apparent power an isotropic source would need to match boresight power density.
items.hint: Transmit power times gain, minus line loss.

items.prompt: An antenna has directivity 20 dBi and radiation efficiency of 80%. What is its gain?
items.explanation: $80\%$ efficiency is $10\log_{10}(0.8) \approx -0.97$ dB. So $G = 20 - 0.97 \approx 19$ dBi. Efficiency subtracts from directivity to give gain.

items.prompt: A Yagi is rated 12 dBd. What is its gain in dBi?
items.explanation: $\text{dBi} = \text{dBd} + 2.15$ because the dipole reference itself has $2.15$ dBi gain. So $12 + 2.15 = 14.15$ dBi.

items.prompt: A transmitter outputs 10 W (40 dBm), the feed line loses 2 dB, and the antenna gain is 25 dBi. What is the EIRP?
items.explanation: $\text{EIRP} = 40 - 2 + 25 = 63$ dBm. EIRP is the boresight power referenced to an isotropic radiator.

items.prompt: For a radar, a narrower HPBW on the main lobe primarily improves:
items.explanation: Narrower beamwidth means the radar can distinguish targets that are closer together in angle, improving angular resolution. It also raises gain, but the direct effect asked here is resolution.

lessons.title: Aperture, Impedance, Polarization & Reciprocity
lessons.content:
On receive, an antenna intercepts power over an **effective aperture** $A_e$. Aperture and gain are locked together by:

$$A_e = \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi} G$$

This is the receive side of the radar/Friis equation. For aperture antennas (dishes, horns) $A_e = \eta_{ap} A_{phys}$, so a bigger dish or higher frequency yields more gain. Equivalently, beamwidth shrinks as aperture grows: HPBW $\approx \lambda/D$ (radians), so **gain, beamwidth, aperture and frequency are one linked story** — the relationship every radar engineer keeps in their head.

**Input impedance** $Z_{in} = R_{in} + jX_{in}$ is what the feed line sees. The real part splits into **radiation resistance** $R_r$ (models radiated power) plus loss resistance. A resonant half-wave dipole has $Z_{in} \approx 73 + j42.5\ \Omega$; trimming it slightly shorter cancels the reactance to give $\approx 73\ \Omega$, a near-ideal match to $75\ \Omega$ coax. A mismatch produces reflections measured as **VSWR** $= (1+|\Gamma|)/(1-|\Gamma|)$; matching networks (from Modules 6-7) and baluns bring it near 1. **Bandwidth** is the frequency range over which VSWR (or gain/pattern) stays acceptable.

**Polarization** is the orientation of $\mathbf{E}$: linear (vertical/horizontal) or circular (RHCP/LHCP). A mismatch between TX and RX polarization costs the **polarization loss factor** PLF $= |\hat{\rho}_t \cdot \hat{\rho}_r|^2$. LP-to-CP costs $3$ dB; cross-polarized LP is a deep null. Polarimetric radar exploits this deliberately.

Finally, **reciprocity**: an antenna's transmit pattern equals its receive pattern. Gain, impedance, and polarization are identical in both directions — so you can characterize an antenna on transmit and trust it on receive.

items.front: State the gain-aperture relationship.
items.back: $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4\pi} G$. Effective aperture and gain are directly proportional; this is the receive side of the Friis/radar equation.
items.hint: Lambda squared over four pi, times gain.

items.front: How does beamwidth relate to aperture size?
items.back: HPBW $\approx \lambda/D$ (radians). Larger aperture or shorter wavelength gives a narrower beam and higher gain. Beamwidth, aperture, gain, and frequency are one linked relationship.
items.hint: Inverse with aperture; proportional to wavelength.

items.front: What is radiation resistance, and what is the half-wave dipole's input impedance?
items.back: Radiation resistance $R_r$ is the equivalent resistance that accounts for radiated power. A resonant half-wave dipole has $Z_{in} \approx 73 + j42.5\ \Omega$; trimmed to resonance it is about $73\ \Omega$.
items.hint: About 73 ohms.

items.front: What is the polarization loss factor (PLF), and what does an LP-to-CP mismatch cost?
items.back: $\text{PLF} = |\hat{\rho}_t \cdot \hat{\rho}_r|^2$, the fraction of power coupled between two polarizations. A linear-to-circular mismatch costs $3$ dB (PLF = 0.5).
items.hint: Dot product of unit polarization vectors, squared.

items.front: State the reciprocity theorem for antennas.
items.back: An antenna's transmit pattern equals its receive pattern; gain, impedance, and polarization are identical in both directions. You can characterize it on TX and trust it on RX.
items.hint: TX pattern = RX pattern.

items.front: What defines an antenna's bandwidth?
items.back: The frequency range over which a chosen parameter (commonly VSWR, but also gain, pattern, or polarization) stays within acceptable limits.
items.hint: Range where VSWR/gain/pattern stay acceptable.

items.prompt: An antenna has a gain of $G = 100$ (linear) at $\lambda = 0.1$ m. What is its effective aperture?
items.explanation: $A_e = \lambda^2 G/(4\pi) = (0.01)(100)/(4\pi) = 1/12.57 \approx 0.080$ m^2. Larger effective aperture means higher gain at a given wavelength.

items.prompt: An antenna presents a reflection coefficient magnitude $|\Gamma| = 0.2$. What is its VSWR?
items.explanation: $\text{VSWR} = (1+|\Gamma|)/(1-|\Gamma|) = 1.2/0.8 = 1.5$. A perfect match gives VSWR = 1.

items.prompt: Because of reciprocity, a radar can use the same antenna for transmit and receive. Which statement is correct?
choices: The receive beamwidth is always wider than the transmit beamwidth
The receive pattern, gain, and impedance equal those on transmit
Reciprocity only holds for circularly polarized antennas
The antenna must be re-tuned between transmit and receive
items.explanation: Reciprocity guarantees identical pattern, gain, impedance, and polarization on transmit and receive (for linear, passive media), which is why monostatic radar reuses one antenna.

items.prompt: A GPS receiver uses an RHCP antenna. Receiving a purely linearly polarized signal of equal power, the polarization loss is about:
items.explanation: A linear wave decomposes into equal RHCP and LHCP components; only the RHCP half couples, so PLF = 0.5, a 3 dB loss. A fully cross-polarized LP-to-LP case, by contrast, is a deep null.

lessons.title: Antenna Types & Selection
lessons.content:
Knowing the menu lets you pick the right radiator for a mission. Working roughly from simple to directive:

- **Dipole** ($\lambda/2$): the workhorse, $\approx 73\ \Omega$, $2.15$ dBi, omnidirectional in the plane perpendicular to the wire, linearly polarized.
- **Monopole** ($\lambda/4$ over a ground plane): the dipole's image-theory half. The ground plane mirrors the missing half, giving $\approx 36\ \Omega$ and doubled directivity. Ubiquitous on vehicles.
- **Loop**: small loops are magnetic-field radiators with low radiation resistance (good for direction finding); resonant loops behave like dipoles.
- **Patch / microstrip**: a metal patch over a grounded dielectric. Low profile, cheap to print, narrow bandwidth, easily arrayed — the backbone of modern phased-array and 5G panels.
- **Horn**: a flared waveguide. Moderate-to-high gain, very clean pattern, wide bandwidth; the standard gain reference and a common dish feed.
- **Parabolic reflector**: a feed at the focus of a dish gives high gain $G \approx \eta\left(\dfrac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2$ with $\eta \approx 0.5$-$0.7$. The classic mechanically-scanned radar dish.
- **Yagi-Uda**: a driven dipole plus parasitic reflector and directors; cheap directional gain (TV, point-to-point).
- **Helix**: a coiled conductor; in axial mode it radiates **circular polarization** along its axis — ideal for satellite and GPS links where Faraday rotation scrambles linear polarization.

For radar, the trade is gain/beamwidth vs steering: a big dish gives a pencil beam but must be mechanically slewed, whereas thousands of patch elements form an AESA that steers electronically (Module 10). Picking an antenna means matching gain, beamwidth, polarization, bandwidth, and packaging to the mission.

items.front: How does a quarter-wave monopole relate to a half-wave dipole?
items.back: By image theory the ground plane mirrors the missing half, so a $\lambda/4$ monopole behaves like half a dipole: about $36\ \Omega$ input impedance and doubled directivity over the upper half-space.
items.hint: Image theory; half the dipole.

items.front: Give the approximate gain of a parabolic reflector.
items.back: $G \approx \eta\left(\dfrac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2$, where $D$ is the dish diameter and aperture efficiency $\eta \approx 0.5$-$0.7$. Gain rises with the square of $D/\lambda$.
items.hint: Efficiency times (pi D over lambda) squared.

items.front: What are the key traits of a microstrip (patch) antenna?
items.back: Low profile, cheap to print on PCB, narrow bandwidth, easily formed into arrays. It is the backbone element of modern phased-array radar and 5G panels.
items.hint: Printed, low-profile, arrayable.

items.front: What polarization does an axial-mode helix produce, and why is that useful?
items.back: Circular polarization along its axis. Useful for satellite/GPS links because Faraday rotation in the ionosphere scrambles linear polarization but not the sense of circular polarization.
items.hint: Think GPS, satellite.

items.front: What is a horn antenna and a typical use?
items.back: A flared waveguide giving moderate-to-high gain, a clean pattern, and wide bandwidth. Common as a standard-gain reference and as the feed for a parabolic dish.
items.hint: Flared waveguide; dish feed.

items.front: Describe a Yagi-Uda antenna.
items.back: A driven dipole plus parasitic elements: one reflector behind and several directors in front. It gives cheap directional gain along the boom; classic for TV and point-to-point links.
items.hint: Driven element plus reflector and directors.

items.prompt: A 1 m dish at 10 GHz ($\lambda = 0.03$ m) has aperture efficiency 0.6. Its gain is closest to:
A link must survive ionospheric Faraday rotation (e.g. a satellite uplink). Which antenna polarization is the best choice?
items.explanation: Faraday rotation rotates the plane of a linearly polarized wave by an unpredictable angle, causing fading. Circular polarization is immune to this rotation, so a helix or CP patch is preferred.

items.prompt: Replacing a mechanically-scanned parabolic radar dish with an AESA of many patch elements primarily buys you:
choices: Higher peak gain than any dish can achieve
Electronic (inertia-free) beam steering and multi-beam agility
A wider instantaneous bandwidth at no cost
items.explanation: The key advantage is electronic beam steering with no moving parts, enabling rapid, agile, and even simultaneous multiple beams. The element-level pattern math is the array factor of Module 10.

items.prompt: Why does a quarter-wave monopole over a perfect ground plane show roughly 36 ohms instead of the dipole's 73 ohms?
choices: The ground plane adds series resistance
It radiates into only half the space for the same current, halving the radiation resistance
The monopole is twice as long electrically
items.explanation: The image in the ground plane completes the equivalent dipole, but the monopole radiates the same power into only the upper half-space for the same input current, so its radiation resistance is about half the dipole's: ~36 ohms.
